title: Local scope

publication.changeSummary: Topic-specific draft for Local scope; requires human source, technical, pedagogy, and accessibility review. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: CS2023 Software Development Fundamentals; August 2023; section applicable to Local scope
sources.claim: Scope determines where a name can be resolved, while lifetime determines how long its associated storage or value exists; a local name normally belongs to its block or call frame.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Scope determines where a name can be resolved, while lifetime determines how long its associated storage or value exists; a local name normally belongs to its block or call frame.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A local variable named total inside calculate does not overwrite an unrelated total in the caller unless an explicit shared object or global is updated. Your goal is to predict name visibility and avoid accidental dependence on global state. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Iteration; Decomposition; Calls, parameters and returns. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Operational model
sections.body: Scope determines where a name can be resolved, while lifetime determines how long its associated storage or value exists; a local name normally belongs to its block or call frame. The learner should use this model to predict an observable result rather than merely repeat terminology.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure point
sections.body: Languages differ in block, function, module, class, closure, and dynamic scope rules, and a captured value may outlive the call that created its local name. A correct explanation therefore includes the applicable assumptions and avoids this common mistake: If two variables share the same spelling anywhere in a program, they must refer to one global value.

includes: Distinguish local names from state visible outside a function.
Predict name visibility and avoid accidental dependence on global state.
excludes: Languages differ in block, function, module, class, closure, and dynamic scope rules, and a captured value may outlive the call that created its local name.

outcomes: Predict name visibility and avoid accidental dependence on global state.

examples.title: Local scope: direct case
examples.situation: A local variable named total inside calculate does not overwrite an unrelated total in the caller unless an explicit shared object or global is updated.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the starting evidence: A local variable named total inside calculate does not overwrite an unrelated total in the caller unless an explicit shared object or global is updated.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.

Step 3 — Check the result against this correction: Resolve each use from its lexical context and active frame, and make cross-scope state explicit rather than assuming identical spelling implies identity.
examples.result: The case supports the declared outcome because Resolve each use from its lexical context and active frame, and make cross-scope state explicit rather than assuming identical spelling implies identity.

examples.title: Local scope: boundary case
examples.situation: Two functions can each use a local index name because each call resolves that name in a separate scope and frame.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed context: Two functions can each use a local index name because each call resolves that name in a separate scope and frame.
Step 2 — Keep this boundary explicit: Languages differ in block, function, module, class, closure, and dynamic scope rules, and a captured value may outlive the call that created its local name.
Step 3 — Reject the documented misconception: If two variables share the same spelling anywhere in a program, they must refer to one global value.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Predict name visibility and avoid accidental dependence on global state.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: If two variables share the same spelling anywhere in a program, they must refer to one global value.
misconceptions.correction: Resolve each use from its lexical context and active frame, and make cross-scope state explicit rather than assuming identical spelling implies identity.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using the direct and boundary scenarios, explain why this belief fails: If two variables share the same spelling anywhere in a program, they must refer to one global value.

analogy.example: A service counter can model Local scope: it advertises accepted requests and results while its internal work remains behind a named interface.
usefulFor: Separating contracts, local work, calls, returned results, and repeated service use
limitations: A service counter does not capture language-specific frames, reference semantics, closures, or recursion limits.

recap.summary: You should now be able to predict name visibility and avoid accidental dependence on global state. Keep this boundary in view: Languages differ in block, function, module, class, closure, and dynamic scope rules, and a captured value may outlive the call that created its local name.
